name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (62)
```

## (Commentary)

The repetition of belief (الإيمان) a second time—which refers to being characterized by its true reality, as suggested by the context—indicates that the intended meaning of "those who believe" (الذين آمنوا) at the beginning of the verse refers to those who are characterized by belief outwardly (ظاهراً) and are identified by this name. Thus, the sum of the meaning is that mere names and being titled with them—such as "the believers" (المؤمنون), "the Jews" (اليهود), "the Christians" (النصارى), and "the Sabaeans" (الصابئون)—do not necessitate a reward from Allah the Exalted, nor do they grant security from punishment. This is contrary to their claim: "None shall enter Paradise unless he be a Jew or a Christian." Rather, the crux of the matter and the cause of honor and felicity is the reality of belief (حقيقة الإيمان) in Allah and the Last Day, and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

For this reason, He did not say "whoever of them believes" by using a pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول) as is required in a relative clause (الصلة), lest it be an affirmation of some benefit in the mere naming, as is evident from the literary structure (النظم). This is among the themes repeated in the verses of the Quran: that felicity and honor revolve around servitude (العبودية) — *the state of absolute submission and devotion to Allah*. No name among these names benefits its bearer in the least, nor does any attribute of perfection remain with its possessor or save him, except through the adherence to servitude. The Prophets and those below them are equal in this regard. Indeed, the Exalted said regarding His Prophets, after describing them with every beautiful attribute: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "Had they ascribed partners [to Allah], what they used to do would have come to naught." (Al-An'am: 88).

The Exalted also said regarding the companions of His Prophet and those who believed with him, despite mentioning their great status and high rank: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا** "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward." (Al-Fath: 29). Thus, He included the word "of them" (منهم). Regarding others who were given the signs of Allah the Exalted, He said: **وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَاهُ بِهَا وَلَٰكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَوَاهُ** "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [low] desires." (Al-A'raf: 176). There are many other verses explicitly stating that honor lies in the reality (الحقيقة) and not the outward appearance (الظاهر).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Narrated from Salman al-Farsi, who said: "I asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the people of a religion I was with; I mentioned their prayer and worship, and then it was revealed: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا** 'Indeed the faithful, the Jews...'" (the verse).

**I say:** The revelation of this verse concerning the companions of Salman has also been narrated through several other chains.

In *al-Ma'ani*: From Ibn Faddal, who said: "I said to al-Rida عليه‌السلام: 'Why were the Christians (النصارى) named *Nasara*?' He replied: 'Because they were from a village called Nazareth (ناصره) in the land of Syria, where Mary and Jesus settled after their return from Egypt.'"

**I say:** There is a discussion regarding this narration that we shall address in the stories of Jesus عليه‌السلام in Surah Al 'Imran, Allah willing.

It is also narrated that the Jews (اليهود) were named *Yahud* because they are from the descendants of Judah (يهودا), the son of Jacob.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: He said: He عليه‌السلام said: "The Sabaeans (الصابئون) are a people who are neither Magians, nor Jews, nor Christians, nor Muslims; they worship the stars and the planets."

**I say:** This is a form of paganism (الوثنية), except that idol worship is not limited to them; rather, what distinguishes them is the worship of idols representing the planets.

## (Historical Discourse)

Abu Rayhan al-Biruni mentioned in *al-Athar al-Baqiyah* the following text: "The first of those mentioned among them—meaning the false prophets—is Budhasaf (يوذاسف). He appeared after one year of the reign of Tahmuras in the land of India and brought the Persian script. He called to the creed of the Sabaeans, and a great multitude followed him. The Pishdadian kings and some of the Kayanids who inhabited Balkh used to venerate the two luminaries (the sun and moon), the planets, and the elemental wholes (كليات العناصر), sanctifying them until the appearance of Zoroaster after thirty years of the reign of Vishtaspa. The remnants of those Sabaeans are in Harran, attributed to their location, so they are called the Harranians. It has been said that the name is attributed to Hadan bin Tarakh, the brother of Abraham عليه‌السلام, and that he was among their leaders, the most deeply involved in the religion and the most steadfast in adhering to it.

Ibn Sankala the Christian recounted from them in his book—in which he intended to refute their sect and thus filled it with lies and falsehoods—that they say Abraham عليه‌السلام only left their community because he developed leprosy on his foreskin, and whoever had that was considered impure and they would not associate with him. So he cut off his foreskin for that reason—meaning he was circumcised—and entered one of the houses of the idols. He heard a voice from an idol saying to him: 'O Abraham, you left us with one defect and came back to us with two; leave and do not return to us.' Rage drove him to break them into pieces, and he left their community. Then he regretted what he had done and intended to sacrifice his son to the planet Jupiter, according to their custom of sacrificing their children—so he claimed. When the planet Jupiter recognized the sincerity of his repentance, it ransomed him with a ram.

Abd al-Masih ibn Ishaq al-Kindi recounted about them in his response to the letter of Abd Allah ibn Isma'il al-Hashimi, that they are known for sacrificing humans, but that is not possible for them today openly. We only know of them that they are a people who profess the oneness of Allah (يوحدون الله) and declare Him transcendent (ينزهونه) above all foul things. They describe Him through negation (السلب) rather than affirmation (الايجاب), such as saying: 'He is not limited, He is not seen, He does not wrong, and He does not act unjustly.' They call Him by the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) metaphorically, as they believe He has no real attributes (صفة بالحقيقة). They attribute the governance (التدبير) of the world to the celestial sphere and its bodies, and they believe in their life, speech, hearing, and sight. They venerate the lights. Among their remnants is the dome above the *mihrab* near the *maqsura* of the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus; it was their place of prayer. The Greeks and Romans were upon their religion, then it fell into the hands of the Jews, who made it their synagogue, then the Christians overcame them and turned it into a church until Islam and its people came and took it as a mosque. They had temples and idols named after the sun with known shapes, as mentioned by Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi in his book on houses of worship, such as the temple of Baalbek which belonged to the idol of the sun, and Qarran, which is attributed to the moon, its construction being in the shape of a *taylasan* (a hooded cloak). Near it is a village called Salamsin, its ancient name being *Sanam Sin*, meaning the idol of the moon, and another village called *Tar' 'Uz*, meaning the gate of Venus. They mention that the Kaaba and its idols belonged to them, and its worshippers were among them; that al-Lat was in the name of Saturn, and al-'Uzza in the name of Venus. They have many prophets, most of whom were Greek philosophers like Hermes the Egyptian, Agathodaimon, Walis, Pythagoras, and Babaswar, the maternal grandfather of Plato, and their likes. Some of them prohibited fish for fear of it being foam, and chicks because they are always feverish, and garlic because it causes headaches and burns the blood or the semen which is the foundation of the world, and fava beans because they dull and corrupt the intellect and because they first grew in a human skull. They have three prescribed prayers.

**The first:** At sunrise, eight *rak'ahs*.

**The second:** At the decline of the sun from the middle of the sky, five *rak'ahs*. In every *rak'ah* of their prayer, there are three prostrations. They perform supererogatory prayers (يتنفلون) in the second hour of the day and another in the ninth hour of the day.

**The third:** In the third hour of the night. They pray in a state of purity and ablution (وضوء), and they perform the ritual bath (يغتسلون) after sexual impurity (الجنابة). They do not circumcise, claiming they were not commanded to do so. Most of their laws regarding marriage and legal punishments (الحدود) are like the laws of the Muslims, and regarding impurity upon touching the dead and such matters, they are similar to the Torah. They have offerings (قرابين) related to the planets, their idols, and their temples, and sacrifices performed by their priests and diviners, through which they extract knowledge of what might happen to the one making the offering and answers to what is asked. Hermes is sometimes called Idris, who is mentioned in the Torah as Enoch, and some of them claimed that Budhasaf is Hermes.

It has been said that these Harranians are not the true Sabaeans; rather, they are those named in books as the *Hunafa* and the pagans (الوثنية). For the Sabaeans are those who remained in Babylon among the tribes that rose in the days of Cyrus and Artaxerxes to Jerusalem; they inclined toward the laws of the Magians and leaned toward the religion of Nebuchadnezzar. Thus, they adopted a path that was a mixture of Magianism and Judaism, like the Samaritans in Syria. Most of them are found in Wasit and the rural lands of Iraq in the region of Ja'far, al-Jamida, and Nahray al-Silah, attributing themselves to Enos the son of Seth. They oppose the Harranians and criticize their doctrines, agreeing with them only in a few things, to the extent that they face the North Pole in prayer while the Harranians face the South. Some of the People of the Book claimed that Methuselah had a son other than Lemech named Sabi, and that the Sabaeans were named after him. Before the appearance of religious laws and the emergence of Budhasaf, people were *Shaminiyyun*, inhabitants of the eastern side of the earth, and they were idol worshippers. Their remnants are now in India, China, and the Tughuzghuz, and the people of Khorasan call them *Shamnan*. Their traces, temples (بهاراتهم), idols, and shrines (فرخاراتهم) are visible in the frontiers of Khorasan adjacent to India. They believe in the eternity of time (قدم الدهر), the transmigration of souls (تناسخ الأرواح), and the falling of the celestial sphere in an infinite void, which is why it moves in a circle, for a circular thing, when displaced, descends with rotation—so they claimed. Some of them acknowledged the temporal creation of the world (حدوث العالم) and claimed its duration is one million years." (End of the relevant excerpt).

**I say:** What he attributed to some—interpreting the Sabaeans as a sect mixed from Magianism and Judaism along with elements from the Harranians—is most consistent with what is in the verse, for the apparent context suggests that the enumeration is of the people of established creeds (أهل الملة).
